explanations.8: This imports Counter, which is a dictionary-like tool that automatically counts how many times each item appears in a list.
explanations.30: This sets a threshold—if two tag names differ by 3 characters or fewer AND one is contained in the other, the code will suggest merging them.
explanations.34: This is a set of tag pairs that are similar but should NOT be suggested for merging, like 'php' and 'phpcbf' which are legitimately different tools.
explanations.50: This function tells the YAML library how to format lists when writing to files—it uses the compact [item1, item2] style instead of a multi-line format.
explanations.54: This function controls how text strings are written to YAML files, using a special pipe character style for multi-line strings and plain style for single lines.
explanations.64: These lines register the custom formatting rules above with the YAML library so they apply whenever you save metadata to a file.
explanations.75: StringIO is like an invisible file in memory where we can build up text before writing it to an actual file.
explanations.86: The function gets the YAML text that was written to the invisible file and stores it in a variable.
explanations.104: Counter is a special dictionary that keeps track of how many times each tag appears across all files.
explanations.122: This creates an empty set (a collection that stores unique items) to collect all distinct tags found, treating 'Python' and 'python' as different.
explanations.158: Python's sort is 'stable', meaning when you sort twice, the secondary sort order (by name) is preserved—this achieves multi-level sorting.
explanations.177: This list comprehension filters to find only groups of tags that have multiple spelling variations (like 'Python', 'python', 'PYTHON').
explanations.182: This creates an empty set to store pairs of tags that the code thinks should probably be merged together.
explanations.191: This creates a canonical order (alphabetically sorted) of the lowercase tag pair so that ('php', 'phpcbf') and ('phpcbf', 'php') are treated as the same pair.
explanations.224: This calculates how many characters different two tag names are—for example, 'snippet' (7 chars) vs 'snippets' (8 chars) has a difference of 1.
explanations.332: The pipe symbol here means the type is either a string OR None (absence of a value), allowing the code to represent 'no old tag' with None.
explanations.356: This checks if the new tag (in lowercase) is already in the list to avoid accidentally adding the same tag twice under different capitalizations.
explanations.362: Before adding a tag to the new list, this ensures a case-insensitive duplicate doesn't already exist (e.g., 'Python' and 'python' count as the same).
explanations.444: os.sys.argv is the list of command-line arguments passed to the script; checking if its length is 1 means the user ran the script with no arguments.
